speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Anna Bengston Thomason and Oscar Thomason took place at their home on Dry Ridge between Troy and Deary on February 3, 1976. That we were born in Sweden? Where was it?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He asked. And he was here. But we asked. After hard of hearing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I'm asking where it was that you were born.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I was just born where I was raised after that in Svenneby called the little place and that was close to Torsby in Varmland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were born just a few miles from there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: From there I was born six and a half Swedish miles north. Swedish miles from the Norwegian border. Where about? Sweden. And they come in from Finland. 1640. And they settled all over up in the hills, both sides of the border. And it was 1640 they come in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were from Finland before?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they come right from Finland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were Finns and not Swedish people?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They were mixed with probably some Swedish Finn. What do you say? Sweden. Finland was belong to Sweden I think around 600 years. In 1917 and 18, after the First World War, the Finn took it from Sweden. And in 1917 and 18, the Finnish people get away from Russia. Then Russia had to get them independent. There was kind of one way of revolution.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well do you think that your own great great great great grandparents come from Finland in the same time?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they did. They come from Finland. On my dad's side. Where I come from, it's all old Finnish name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true for Anna's place too or was hers not?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no there was not. It was not that far down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't come then?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, that moved south.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how did the people get along with the Finns where you lived?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They get along with all of them was Finn. They come from Finland. And they was all up in the hills. And they could cut down trees. And they burned trees in the forest. Then they seeded rye. You know what rye is? They seeded that. And they built up in the woods. But later on, you know, the company, they don't like to see they done that. They took the land back when they couldn't live there and use it. And then they had to pay a little tax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the company?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: That was their, they call it, well we company, they call it, they call it Isvala Bulog. What they call it? Bulog is the same as company, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was owned by some rich Swedish people?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, they were rich by the year. There was a company. Like now we can call it Potlatch Lumber Company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Same kind of thing.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, but it was. So then they, and you have to pay a little bit tax. That was the very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well your family, when you grew up there, they were pretty poor weren't they? They didn't have much money did they?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no, they were very much money. And they worked, they grew up, and my dad, they worked for a company. And they don't pay very much. So that was the most war.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the same when most of the families, most of the people were poor there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: There was some. There was some that owned the land and they owned a lot of timber, see? Timberland. Well, there was. The company bought them out, see? Gave them pretty good price and there was a timber on the, there was no price on the timber, see? So they thought it was very cheap. After the company gave it. Then, look out, the price went up. Of course wasn't it that way that, it was good for some of them that they didn't have a home because this company used to have places for them to stay. Well they had, you know, in the company, they kind of built the houses on them, see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like here, it sounds like here with potlatch. The same thing they bought the timber from the people.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they bought the timber from the people. So that helped them though, that they had a place to live. And the tax we paid was very low. When there was poor land, lot of rocks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was no farming to speak of there.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We had never seen rocks like it was there, even in the road. No, when they had a better road. Even if the road was made up, the road went over. It was covered with land there. I walked there and I could have failed ten times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Trying to walk in the road? Just walking in the road.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: One day on the county road, they walk on a big rock in them. We understand we call it highway, and we call it land sway over there. Then they would take the big rock in them. Now they have much better road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were just a child when you were talking about you were walking on the road. Just a kid. Yeah, maybe so.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We were visiting his relatives and we went someplace. I didn't know what. We went to visit some of his friends. I thought I stumbled on those roads. There were those rocks and they weren't big. But the road wasn't smooth. That's what I know. You remember, when you lived out in Westdale, you know the colors of rhino. They wanted a rock in that road. Big, big rock. Yeah, it was a rock. And they drove with one horse and a wagon. And you know they hit the rock to your house. So it must... But the head highway, that come not far from where he lived. It wasn't far.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there farming where your family lived? Was there much farming there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yes, it was farming. I don't say we had a big place, but it was farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did your family have?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Land, I know that. You can call it used to be about 5th, probably 28th. When there was pretty good land, they would never see. When they were dead, around there, there were some really big farmers. We had a neighborhood, they called it, they had lots of land. And then on the other side of us, on the highway, you know, in the weekends, a big lake. It was a big farm. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your father make a living? Could he make a living from farming that little bit of land?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He was a carpenter. He built houses. Carpenters? No, we... We argued maybe we could because we had cows and had our milk and we raised pigs and things like that, had their chickens. But that wouldn't have been... He couldn't make living. So we were so many kids too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many kids in the family?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Eight in our family. Of course, my oldest brother, he went there before the youngest was born. So we weren't dead at one time. So I know my dad was out working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were just a child growing up, what was the house like? Did you have much room in the house? Did all the kids sleep in one room or what?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He had this big... Hope he had a big kitchen, I'll tell you. And we had rooms, though. Not small room and so on. He had a good house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father build it? No.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I'm sure Papa didn't build that. No, no. But I was born and raised there. My oldest brother... How old was your old... Werner was born there, the first one. That was the third in the family. He was born there and there we lived. So... And after that, all the rest of us, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hjalmar came to the United States when he was just a young man.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Most of my brothers went here when they were young. They weren't very old. Hjalmar came here in 1905. But he come here... My mother had a brother in Idaho. And he went there. And then after one went, then it was easy for the other to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your mother's brother ever come back to Sweden to talk about what it was like here? Did you just find out from letters?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I didn't know. I was quite young. But he was back there, oh yes. He asked about your mother's brother if he come back to Sweden. And you know your uncle Sven come back. Yeah, that was my brother's brother. He asking if he said something about America around here how it was here. Hjalmar did like to the old...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm just wondering how Hjalmar found out or decided he should come here. If uncle told him.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: America was Hjalmar's... So he thought a lot of America. How he find out that he went here. Yeah, you find out before you come here from Hjalmar. Things were... He got you started. His uncle was here, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they say about America?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yeah, I can't explain exactly. There was things he told us. He felt good about America. I can't say anything else because it was too bad he couldn't come back. Because he sure thought a lot of America and Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hjalmar couldn't come back? Why was that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: His wife didn't come. He married. One time when he was home he married and she didn't want to come. And he come back here because he thought maybe she will come but she didn't. So he went home of course. So that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to her, his wife, about why she wouldn't come?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no, I never did. No, I never did. Not that. But he sure... But it was the same with Papa. He went there and thought Mama will come. But he even had the ticket and all. But she didn't come either. It was just the same for Papa. It was for Hjalmar. And both of my brother and my dad went home. And the wife didn't want to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He even bought her a ticket?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, they said that I'm Papa and I never discuss it. But I heard they said that because it was long before I was there. But they said that they bought the ticket but she didn't want to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that women want to come to leave Sweden? Not just your mother didn't and your sister-in-law didn't, but why would they want to stay? Why would women think it was better?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't know. I don't know. You're afraid of order.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For what?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Afraid of order. To go over the ocean. I don't know. I think your mother bought. Oh yeah, my wife did. That was a long time ago. It took a long time. You have to go on the boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think maybe they didn't want to give up their friends?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, like for Hjalmar's wife, her parents were getting old. So that had to do with it too. Because she had a brother-in-law that stayed home with my mother and daddy. But I don't know if Maria ever wanted to go anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it must have made it hard for the families, you know, not to be separated like that.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I tried it so I know about this. Even the head of me. But for me to go to America, that was the difference because I had so many of my family. You had so many brothers that went over there. So they were kind of hard for your mother and even your dad. And they left all of them. Yeah, one after another left. My dad never, that I know, talked to the boys. They were the first ones that they should go. I don't know why. He took everyone to the hospital, put them on the train there. And that was something he did. But he was the way he lived. A thinking man too. So I guess maybe he thought it would be better for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was after he'd gone back to Sweden to stay.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, oh yes, he was in Sweden. And he worked hard too. Of course, when he was here in America, it was in cars and things so he couldn't tell them anything about that either. Because I don't know if it ever was a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, Anna, what do you think that people heard as a rule about America? What did people in Sweden think that America was going to be like?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, of course, a long, long time ago I heard the saying that it used to cut gold with a knife. It wasn't any bad things. And when they talk about that, the Wild West, you know. Some afraid, you know, they come to them. Call it the Wild West, you know, you see. Indian and stuff. They call them the red skin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Sweden, people knew about that.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They heard about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the Wild West supposed to be like?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, the Wild Indians had to fight them. No, it was. Oh my. They were talking about that. They talked about it. And sometimes when they wrote, it was funny, they wrote the Wild West. The Wild West. Not on the address outside, they wrote, but they quit. Because the name and the Wild West sometimes to be fun. No, of course, Papa was here. And I think he would have rather see the kids come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because there was more opportunity, was that why?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, Papa had thought, had the right thinking. But he didn't decide for anyone. Yes, there was more opportunity here to make it, you know. To raise a family. But then, of course, Papa worked here. For farmers back east. And he said it was shocking green here. And he said it was hot. It wasn't so hot in Sweden. And he said, I am with those delicious cells. This was pouring. So he knew what that was. Then he was in Seattle before he decided to come home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have been pretty disappointed that your mother wouldn't come. I guess.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He was a man that wouldn't have said anything to Mama. So that she wouldn't have heard of her. But my brothers in Seattle didn't know where Papa had a lot. He bought that, but then when he decided to go home, that he left there for taxes. So that, and the boys, my brothers, go where it was. It's too, just about middle of Seattle. So you see that was, but yeah. But yeah, he worked and he was

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up, what did they teach you that girls should know to learn how to do?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, I don't think there was anything more than before the long, I don't think so. If I ever go into school, that's what I would have wanted to go into. But I couldn't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? You started school and then you had to quit?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, it was an ordinary grad school. I couldn't, Mama was sick. And I had three brothers younger than I was. And Papa had to go out to work. And of course, so that also I asked if I could be home then. And the art teacher, we knew him real well. So he knew how it was. But I can't see how teacher could do that, those days. Let me out of school. But he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What grade were you in then?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I was 12. I really don't know what to call the grades there. But I had six weeks in the winter that we always had to repeat the years. And that, of course, I had to go to. And that was pretty rough too because we were supposed to know quite a bit when we are going through those years. So, but then that I had to take. But I was 12 when I was really out. But not, no, six weeks I had left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you sorry at the time that you had to leave school?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, of course, I didn't want to go to school. With the kids and life. But I had plenty to do, so I was busy. It was a good thing I was strong. It was really too hard many times. Be out in the fields and work and do things. But, of course, I was too heavy. I know that, but we didn't think about it. I guess we were hungry and we ate and put on weight. That was the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you had to do the field work too.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, the women always stayed. So we held, but that, of course, I didn't drive any horses or anything. I didn't do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do in the field?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, when it was haying, of course. And they hung up the hay there on rails. They put down, I was going to explain post rooms, and then the rails there. And when they hayed, they had to hang up the hay to get it right. Where I come from, it was a different place. So that was to carry that hay. Papa used to stand there by that thing and rake the nuts of the bus straighten out and lay it up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to carry big bundles of hay?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, my brothers and I, we used to carry over to him that way. And then when it got to, of course, if we had had, it was a big place, maybe. Maybe they would have done cupping more like they do here. But we had a small place and we did it mostly by hand. And then, of course, when they cupped the right, the women was there making the bundles and with the oars and things like that. That's what we did. So on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tie the rod with twine?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, not with twine. We took it, took it, it's a man and, and saw it down with the saw. And it, they were good. Some of those men that lay so nice to pick up. Papa was good. Oscar stayed. I was there one time and tied those bundles. He was very good. He was now the younger ones. I don't think could have done that, to do that first. So we took it up. Went and took it up and we had enough for a bundle. We lay it down. Then we took so many straws of that and made a twist on it. And then we divided it and took around that, the buck we had laying there. And then we twist it like that and then we tuck it under. That's why. That's why. How did you do that? Maybe, just a little sit there. Oh, I see an eye in the bush. We had company. I had a little girl here. This former was visiting me, a neighbor girl. She helped me so many things. She's three, she's been between you. She's so sweet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the kids, when you were being brought up over in Sweden, do you think it's very different from the way the kids are brought up now?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, no. It isn't that way now there in Sweden either. Oh, I guess they are without helping in the fields or so. But that's not, that wasn't, I don't know. I think they can, they have more help. They have lots to do out in the field because my youngest brother's wife had married with the Swedish girl and she helped them there. And they were too, at least in the tired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to work around the house very much too, Anna, when your mother was sick? Did you have to do the house? Oh, yes.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: And my little brother, of course, he always called on me. But it was an actual thing. Oh, no, I don't, do you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean you cooked meals?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, no, but you know, we were young kids. All that had to be the way it was. But we cooked and we, none of us started over. There was one of my brothers next to the youngest. He was thin. The best of us were heavy otherwise. But he was thin and I don't know why because he was, so we cannot work for him because he was more thin. But he's all right. He lives in Seattle and he's all right. Or we can talk about this. Oh, yes. No, and the boys and I had all those boys. But we get along good, I don't know. So I always thought the one that had a sister was the luckiest. Also lucky if like I didn't have any sister. But the boys and I, we get along too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were the only girl? The only girl in the family?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: The only girl in the family. So, but like I say, they were...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you must have had to do a lot of the kitchen work?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yes, I did that. But like I say, we, like I say, the boys got, was good too to get old for me and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you come in the family as far as the order of the kids? Were you the...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I have to think there was three younger and I was. And it was eight, so I must be in the fifth one that way. And Willa be the fourth. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother sick for a long time?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, she was sick for a long time. So we never, we got used to that. I mean, we never expect her to do... Oh, sometimes she was up when we were out in the haying and worked sometimes she was up cooking coffee for us. We were eating on that afternoon coffee and things like that. So she was up in between and helped too. But it was a young mother, wife's mother, she said that her kids, they had, we had three kids and then all of us was about shoulder, she had those three kids and they were poor to eat and she fussed with them and all, tried to cook the first things so they would eat. And she said about us kids, they'll run and make themselves take themselves bread and butter and their fats. And she struggled with her food, to try to get something to sedate them so they would eat. So I guess we just worked and we were never fussed with. That's for sure we were. We had good appetite and that's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, some of the proverbs, the sayings that you were telling to Edith, some of those sayings that are, one of them is about Maria's stinginess. Remember that, who was stingy? And this, I wrote it down, there was... There's no end to Maria's stinginess, is what it is.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, that stinginess is what it does mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Being, not being generous, being tight.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh no, that wasn't in our home though. That should be stingy though, even though we didn't have everything in overflow to overflow. But that we had, we had always plenty of food and things like that, that we had. So we, yeah, we can, we can... We have been back to Sweden. Oscar and I was back in 48. And then my brother and his wife and Oscar and I, what year was it we were there? Sixty-seven. Sixty? But I say, I have liked America and America being good to me. I like to be in America. I do. That's for sure. Everyone was so friendly and so good to me when I came. And yes, they loved, everyone's beloved. So I like America. But I say if I should, for some reason, have to go on as the only fuss with that thing, that works. But our editor is here. That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up in your family, did you kids have to go out and start working very young?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, they were young. And there was work in the woods. That's all it was. There was no factory, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you started to work there in the woods?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I was with my dad, I think, around nine years old, in the woods when I couldn't do very much. When I had an axe, and he fought a tree for me, I can remember, and then you have to peel. So one day I peeled trees, you know, those small trees, and I had to use the axe. Otherwise you wouldn't use a spud. Didn't they use a saw there to cut down trees? No, they used a saw to saw them down. I couldn't tell them. I was never out cutting trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father was working for the...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't know, I can't see if I have any.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was working for the company then?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: For the company. He owned the land. But my dad, he owned the building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He owned the house?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He owned the house and the building and everything. And it all was made of logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he could just barely make a living. The company didn't give too much money for the work. No.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Maybe they never worked for them, you know. They don't have hardly no money over. And then you have to sign the kunzah. And you have to get them anywhere else. Otherwise they'll fine you. For some of the timber somewhere left in the woods, you know, you have to pay for that. You don't pay for the whole value. You pay for each tree somewhere down. And you have to pay for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he work by himself with his family? Your father?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, sometimes there were two. One other had a horse, you know, and they started in the fall. They cut the paper wood, some of them, into the paper mill. When they made lumber, you can't cut them too early for the dry yard. So you cut them later on. When there's some paper wood, you can cut all summer. So I don't dare to say as soon as they get a little older, they end up off to the roof. It's very difficult. Hard, hard work. And then they work it in the log drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A log drive on the river?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I remember I had a bone in the 75. I remember the bone crown in 75 cents, by any way you can call it. They wouldn't call it this, cent. That all worked pretty good. And I don't get them any years. I had them four or five years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work on the river drive? What did you do on that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You see, the harder logs in the wintertime, they deck them up. And they put skid in between. And they were up pretty high. I think they were up to 20 feet high. Right where the river was. And then they rode them in the river. And there was a dam. They opened up the dam. When they opened, they took the logs. They kept on going. There were many dams.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work on the river to get the logs loose? Did you have to do that too?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, but they could be. It was so far from the river. And they were what happened to some to be in the ice. You have to cut them off. I worked on the river in the winter of 1917. And decked them up, you see, there were two of us. And then we had to come and scale them, you see. Then you had to put the marker out. For a different company. They come down to the big lake. There they were desorbed. They were people who had done that. When that come to a longer, well, three to six feet is mouth, the big lake comes for another day, you see. That lake was big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to go to school very much?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Before? No. We had school in the spring and in the fall. And I think there was only two months at a time. In the wintertime, we had to go to the rock. Kids had to walk or a base. The schools, for me, I lived close to school. And then no hot nurses. You see, we had to take her and my mom's kids coming. And they had a little milk pub, milk tea, and then sandwiches. Because it was not easy. Cold, you know, milk, cold, you know. The bar moved a little bit in the school house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the school like to learn?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, there were benches like they had here. We had the long benches, you know. We sit forward in each bench. And there was a pulpit, you know, where the teacher was sitting, you know, and talking. And we had a bad teacher. It was a man teacher. And he was drinking. He was 50 years old when he died. Big man, he had been sick for, I think, over two years. And then we get other teachers, and there was a lady teacher. And they were nice, the learners. That man, not learners. He expected to know everything. And he hit us. His lap was pretty hard. It was not easy to go to school. Then we come home, we took the books home and then we have to read. In the evening. And then we have to know it by heart. They were really off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were really off.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: So this is something we made up. If we get out of the school, that teacher is going to get it. And then he gets sick and he died. I think we've been taking it. Beat him up. We beat us up so bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the town that you grew up in?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, there was really a town. There was a store in Post Office. There were two stores in Post Office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it have a name?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They called it the county. They called it Södra Finnskoga. You say Finn. They come in. They put them there for that. Finnskoga. And then they had the first period come, they were Nieskubba. When they come, they talk. You can hear them, they come from that county. They were talking more like Finn. I can hear that and they were home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many families lived right around the store in that area?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We was, I can't prove it. One, two, three. One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine. There were just about ten families right there. And they were schooled in school. But then just about 15, 20 minutes from there, there were three in the woods, there were seven families. And that was all the directions. And they come to the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the families, were they sort of together in groups, the houses?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, yes, yes. They were in a group. Yes, they were. We knew all the people there, around there, even in the Norwegian border.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Knew all the families?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: All the families, we knew. And they had a name on the house and things that they lived in. So we know the first name on the people. And the second one, last name we don't know, honey. Know them all right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had their first name on the house.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: First name, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the families poor too, just working for the company?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they worked for the company, except they owned their own land. They had little bevel. But they worked for the company too, and so probably sometimes they sold timber to them. They were much paid, very little. So we had used the school to be in the company, they owned the land, you see. We always get work first. And it was properly taxed, but pretty small, you see. They don't show very much, the company.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your grandparents and their parents, had they always lived in that area? Yeah. Around there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they did, see. So my mother, you know, she was born in the woods. There were three families who come in there and settled down. And there was my grandparents on my mother's side. And there was even, on my mother's side there was two boys. And four girls, six. And there were four of them that went over here. They went to Duluth, Minnesota. Then one went back. Here it is, for your camera. I'm going to turn it. I'll use this as your snapshot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, where's that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He can hand us to speak. Or the depot. The depot there. You can take care of that. We have other names already, from the time we went home. ... now, and he was going to forgetful when we were home. He's my godfather. Oh yeah, and we had to go there and he forgot what he said, Anna. Are you Anna? He said, are you home from America? And he asked me so many, many times. And of course he was my godfather. He went by and was baptized, of course, his wife. And they did that there. They had someone to take them, take the kids. And he never, all through years we lived with neighbors and all that. But that was something, he was my godfather.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was important, the godfather was important in...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, of course we were used to it, but he taught that. So he sure did talk a lot to me because he was my godfather. So I took that picture and that's what I was going to explain. I had to see the horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they, was he picked to be your godfather because he was a good friend of your parents? Yeah. Is that why? Because he was good friends?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, we were neighbors. We were neighbors and of course sometimes, yeah, like I was the only girl, you know, they had to pick some special... Well I was bad, I was among all the boys. So I didn't know that. And I was old enough to know that he was my godfather, but he sure... Sometime on Christmas morning when we came from shirts and we walked with kids, he stopped and said, and I was with the Berns, too, and you come and right now you get the ride home. So he always was kind of special to me because he was my godfather. Here is a nice place. It was a good man, but it was nothing other than we were home. It was a forgetful place. Here are the neighbors we had last year. Oh, yes. Oh, that's pretty. Beautiful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the church a separate building from the school? There was a separate building for church?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, the church was two serious models, I believe. The church, you see. And then we went out to school. We had to go and get confirmed, the lieutenant, you see. And we had to go. Soon the week later, and I went 42 times. On Friday, I went Friday morning, then he was studying. No, I went Saturday morning. I was like, Saturday morning and I was studying Saturday. And Sunday we went to go to church and after the service, sometimes we keep us after, we have to study them. So we stayed overnight in one place, you know. There were three boys of us. The girl was upstairs. The man we stayed with, you know, he was a butcher. And he had the rigs. He went out and sold meat. One time, the girl came down, you see, and we were used to laying down in the blanket house, you know, and the old man was home. He went upstairs. He went up and brought them up. He said, that was my right, that was for you through the window. The girl never came back anymore, that way, you know. They never come to talk about it, you see. No, that man never did, you know. He said in the morning, he said, them boys was all right, then my damn girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would stay overnight on Saturday night?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, on Saturday night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, stay overnight.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Then we have to go to the meeting, the church, and then sometimes we keep us after to study. Study the Bible.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Then we were down there, but on the orbit of the I had one crown at 25 cents, you can call it, and it was 250 for a kilo of side pork, you see, but I don't have money, so I had about half a kilo, and they have to last all week. It was all... You don't get much see. Then we grew up, we got big and we got tough. There was nothing, when we got sick there was no doctor. We have to lay in the week and six to week, we lay in the bed, and then we got out too, you know. We got the flu, you see, many times. And that was... What we had when we were kids, we were skiing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For fun?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, for fun. And we ski all day. Remember, I come home, it was cold, you know, and feel cold, wet, and you know, so my mother would come out and help to get my ski out, you see. We had some time too, and they went all right, and we did it. Even after I grew up, many days I worked in the rain, you know, when there was snow and things, when I worked, and then we went in. And I don't adhere to it. We did. We kept farm working, you see, and then home we had real good clothes, you know, even we had real good homemade, you know, in the wool clothes, you see. When we get wet anyway, when the snow was beyond, you know, they went through, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were learning this, learning the Bible, was there... They expected you to learn a lot. They did.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You see, we went to school, we had one day, one hour every day, we studied there, at the Kismet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In school.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: In school. And we had to learn it by heart. And then we went and confirmed there was the Bible. And we went over the Bible too. The Reverend, the pastor, you know, he took over there, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, all the boys that were staying, were those your brothers that were staying too, in the house? No.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Or just other friends? No, there were two who went to school with, there were three.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: There were three of us. But I tell you, them girls, you know. And I always had a girl that never talked to me. I say, that's all them, you see. She was one of them. She lived right where I could talk to the boys, 1924. Like, only two. Nor me. And she was there, where? Here in work, you know. You don't got neighbors, you never said no. Did you take cream in your car? No, just black. Black car?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just black.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Some years ago we never asked that because everybody took cream. But now, so many dead things. This would be nice. Sometimes you can make a trip over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I would love to see it.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: A lot of things, you know, like you, you know, you can have other things to talk about, a lot of things to write down. You can take that trip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it only once that this girl, that girl came downstairs?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: All four of them, all four. All four? Yeah, well, the old man was home and they don't know that. Anybody still never go home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't know that he was home.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, they don't know. We get so scared, shut up myself. He went upstairs and he bawled them out. He said in the morning, I was here, I should throw them through the window. And there was a second story of him. So we had, we, he, and then he told his wife. He was a rough man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he didn't kick you out.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no, we never done anything. And he don't kick the mother or the girls out either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were learning catechism too.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, you bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you tell me that once you went in the front door by mistake? Yes. What was the story there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, there was a story there. I went there and get the papers. I'm going to get the passport. And it was 1918 and I had to go to the, to the, to the kitchen to get the passport. The passport, the ribbon, what do you call it here. And I went in on the front door, see. I was supposed to come go by the way around the house and come in and there's a small kitchen and then he had the office there, see. And well he had the office on Tuesday and Thursday I think you can go see him. And he started to call me out. They come in there. But bigger people you know, people we had a party and things like that. They come in on the other door, come in the front.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The well to do people you mean?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, well the wealthy people, they couldn't go in there but I couldn't. And I don't know. So his wife said, you don't know but they come in and go in there. I told her about it. And he asked me do you think that you, a parent would like to see how you're going to get the pass and go to Norway. Yes I think so I said. He was nineteen, eighteen, seventeen years old. He couldn't ask the men. They didn't deal with all that down there. You see. 1920 I never had a, for the random walk we were four brothers always. And that's what I want to initiate. And we went there, we had no passport. And then the door from the short few go there to see if we had a passport. We had to turn that into the short. So we had to go home, we had to go home and go into a bigger city to get the passport. Richard don't be in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't even tell her. I mean they'd go in the head and do it.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: But who was it that picked her up? No, it was Artus. Artus? He was with the Dickens you know. And they come from Troy and he was in Troy I suppose walking and probably said, oh they're both here. I don't know anybody. And Artus said, you can come closer, sit closer to me. Art played an alumi. Oh, they couldn't be here. Your wife probably don't like it. She was laughing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I heard that she and in fact Ken Sandquist told me that she and Emil had had a fight over the boundary between the place. Yeah, oh yeah. Did you hear that story? He put up a new fence and she was out watching him every step of the way.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I noticed that they used to feel sorry for her sometimes. It was at Christmas time they took the stuff and was going to run over me. And that didn't really happen. Never took it. So she had a right to have funny ideas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you say that they thought that she wouldn't accept, she just wouldn't accept.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, she wouldn't accept that she wouldn't be here. So maybe it was Sylvia that she thought was alright. Well, that's Stanley. I think she wrote with Stanley to Tom. I think he did. She was funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I never heard that by the time she ran off the assessor. That's a new story about her. For me, I didn't hear that one. She really ran him off. She ran the assessor?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: She did. She ran him off and he was the assessor. Took up any of her hair. I know it's like, man, but he eat hair. And took it all the places. And she said, ma, next time you have to have the shirt off. And if the shirt went there and he wasn't with, I don't know, but he went to the mosque. And said he couldn't do nothing with it. So he probably was with them too. Probably he said, I don't know why he couldn't say anything, but he probably was with them. Took down things from the papers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to her?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I never talked to her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because I never could figure out what made her so different, why she didn't like people. It seemed like the people were so friendly around that it seemed to be pretty hard to not like people.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: All of our friendly people, all our neighbors were nice neighbors to you. And I know she was independent, you know. Well, her was, you know, Rosalind. She is on the county road now, you see. He kind of like him, so she bought the place. He got to do it. Yeah, he can buy the place and for a bit of logs. On the right side, he then turned there, you see. Sort of belonged to Matty Harris. And he logged that and then he sold it to Kenneth Sanquist. He sold the place, you know, and he took the bill. But he tried to stop it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But she'd already sold the place.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, only when they see that he was not supposed to sell it to them, Sanquist. No, no, there was a trade. But therefore, there was nothing said in the paper. He bought it, so he sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well that's, she just didn't like him because he was related to Emile.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, he was related to Emile. Oh, I guess so. He was against Emile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Emile was her neighbor and I think that must be why.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I know what they said. They felt sorry for her. They would like to take over things, but they couldn't, they couldn't take things they have made. She had the brothers who stayed with her. And I think that what other women do, women stay there. And that fellow, that could have been a doctor, Emile, women, I don't know, other women, he got cancer. Cancer in his nose and face.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's what I heard.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, he was a man. They're bleeding. Here it is. Men? Men. When they go home, they will die, you know. Maybe she was a meme, so we couldn't go to the doctor. If we weren't going to the doctor right away, we hadn't drove her down, we couldn't fix that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But she didn't, didn't want to...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I don't think, I don't think... This maybe, I think... But he might be cold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't really, that's the time. He didn't really ever get treated for it, did he?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, he never get treated for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the story that I heard was that...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Ah, he got sick very bad. Can they run to his house?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard that after he died, she just left him there in the house and it took a while before they found him.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, they were right, yes. And they had the whole place for kind of eating. What does he have? Cancer. He had a headache. Even when the doctor went to him, he was a little scared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's funny, I guess... I really wonder why she just disliked people. I think she just didn't get along with... I don't know if she just didn't like Swedish people or what.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, that's what it could be. It could be, that's the fact that she don't like this. Emil was a nice man. Oh my goodness. Yeah, he was. When there was a sandwich, they were against him too, you know. Emil was married to the sandwich girl. He didn't like seeing him. And she was in a different clothesline. She had... She had a skirt that went way down to her shoes. I don't know, but I don't know. Maybe layer shock. I've never talked to her. That's stylish now. I said, I talked to her, but I didn't have a place for her. If I'd been talking to her. Maybe. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you didn't tell her who your friends were, maybe.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You know the problem. I'll never thank you for that. She could never, never have gotten better people, the neighbors than she had when she had them. No, even in San Francisco, nice people. That's the way. Couldn't do nothing better, you see. He didn't understand that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I was going to ask you some more about the confirmation. I was talking to Oscar about being confirmed. You were confirmed too, right?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Didn't you go to confirmation?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to learn a lot of the Bible for that too?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yes. When we went to confirmation, we learned because I met them. It wasn't too hard because we had little religion in scope. Not much, but little. We had the family over to the school. We had one hour every day we had them. First thing in the morning. So that kind of helped too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your confirmation classes like? Oh, yeah.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We had one day, one day a week. And then the period we went to confirmation. And the minister was there and asked us questions. And of course, we had to read. And then of course, gave us questions. And we had to kind of have a little bit at home to learn too. And then when we had gone, we went and worked so long. Then of course, we were confirmed. And that looked like we had to go to school. Because maybe some don't belong to the local. Then we had a choice. So I say that it was a kind of, but I don't know how it is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was one story that you told Mrs. Archibald that I saw. That was about the story that they told you about the spirit children who were dancing. What was that story?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I tell you. It was long ago because it was Mama's, my mother's minister. So I know, but she didn't tell it because I guess that minister didn't tell it. Then Mama went to confirmation because it was the same minister. Because Mama, yeah, he said that it was so important to be baptized. That was all it was, to be baptized. But then he told that it was a neighbor lady that told me, her girl and I, we were really good friends over there. And she went to come from Mason for the same minister, Mama. Because I recognized her name. And she told, he told them, they believed. Of course they believed. I'm sure she did believe. Even, I guess the kids were just really scared. That it was, they should be baptized. And the minister come by the cemetery one time, night. Yeah, I don't know if it was really night, but after that, I can't believe it was. I had no way of combining something. And there it was, kids that had showing hands and had a ring there and then the spirit on the cemetery and had some good time. And then others sat there and they couldn't join because they weren't baptized. So the minister went and talked to them and he baptized them. And they baptized them there. And then the other opened up and let them join.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Opened up the circle?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah. Ken, Maddie, Cecily, Lily, that was kids. And of course, at the time, I heard that, you know, we were so used to that. And we, her daughter, we were so surprised and thought it was so terrible. It got old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you didn't believe it when you heard it?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Then I believed it. You did? Of course I believed it then. But I don't believe things like that now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you then when you were getting confirmed?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: When I was told this. Oh, when you were told this. Oh yeah. I can't say exactly but if I say 14, 15 years old or something. And most of it, I don't know. I couldn't say it for sure. I couldn't say it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's when you got confirmed? When you were about that age?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, maybe 15 at least. About 14. You were 14. But they had a time to live so far away and they had to go and stay overnight. Well, look at how they were getting real good fun, you know, with them girls, you know. But listen now. How did you get there? Bicycling? Bicycling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I didn't know that. So when you were a kid, you heard stories like that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, but that was better. We had never heard stories when they were dumb enough to believe them. It wouldn't be a story otherwise. That was it. You have to tell a story. But we were so surprised being a boy and I when we sat there and listened to her mother. The toll on our thought was that that was all the ministers. His point one was that they should be baptized. Because that was just when he, that minister went in there and baptized and he opened up the circle and he can't admit it. It's so dumb. Oh, but they are breathing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me that the people, the ministers were very high up in society?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yes. You can hardly talk to them. No, we were afraid to go talk to them. They brought me up very convenient from the door. They did get this in the book part of this baptized business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know if it was in the magazine story, but it was in the, in what she put in the collection from what you told her. I don't know if that was in there, but it's a good story.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I don't know what. I feel I must tell them that we were so dumb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, were you afraid to talk to the minister too? To the pastor?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no, no, not when we went to confirmation. Of course we couldn't go and talk to him, but of course he had us there and he asked questions and he called anyone he wanted. That old minister got sick before a year was up for us to be confirmed. So they had two. And that's what they did? Yeah, they had two. They had the older ones when they had me being, they started, of course, young when they were through their schooling. And when they got older, they get tired. They kind of moved, I guess they moved them up for a while if they were earned. They moved up like they do in the army. So they, so he was approached, they call it, where I call it. And then the younger ones that, they had two in the church. Then he had to take over when our minister got sick and dies. Otherwise, also many kids in our church, they divided it. Some have, if I say, south from the church and the other north from the church. And this, and then the priest had this up one year and the minister, the younger ones had the north and the next year they changed their minds and said everything was fair. So I had the old one when I started, but he died before we were confirmed. So then of course the younger ones took us all, but he's classless. So.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your confirmation class very big?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, it was big. It was big.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you expecting to tip your hat to the pastor if you saw him on the...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yes, yes. Even in the post office, you took your hat out and you went in. But I remember, we were in Norway here in 1918 and I went in in the post office back then. And he was staring and looking in the window, something had me back against me, he was in a big post office. And that was Norwegian, he told me, you never said hello. And I said, good morning and good evening and something like that. Well, he was a good dog, so he'd be the president then. A good day. Yes, yes. I told him, you had your back against me, so don't you think he told me? Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was Norwegian.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, he was in the post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to take your hat off to the political leaders too or just to the pastor?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: The pastor, and you went in in the bank and you went in the post office, you used to take your hat off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just to go into the bank or the post office? Why?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't know. If you had a cold in the winter, you'd take your gloves off. You'd go in whenever you went in. That old one was Axel Bum, who I was. He came in with a toothpick, in the welllands. Yes, he was in this country of course. And he had a hat on. They told him to take off his hat and I kept it on. He'd been here before. They were telling about one, his name was Roman. And he has been in this country. And he was at home in Sweden and he had logs. And then he was going in the bank and he had those heavy wool mittens. He come in there and he took off his mittens and laid them there when he told what he wanted. And I don't know if they did anything to him. No, he was the big one. He was a rich man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who is this now?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: His name was Roman. He was right home, you see. But he has been in this country. He couldn't see the idea. He come home this morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He took his gloves off?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, laid them on the counter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're not supposed to lay the gloves on the counter.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh no, no. We wasn't supposed to take them in there. I guess I'm not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you supposed to take them off and leave them outside?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason

They took, they don't get very much paid at first, you know, for they have to recognize the union, see, first, see. And then they start to walk, you see. The union, they put it powerfully over there too. I don't know here a union, pretty powerfully, they might go too far too, you know.

words: But over there, when they came in, it sounds like they needed them, they needed to have some.

words: Yes, they needed the union. You know, the log hauler, see, like my dad, long time ago, he had to have a log cart to cut logs. He had to pay them. If he was going to pay so much price, you know, he wouldn't get nothing. That's the point. When after that the union come in, the company have to pay them log cart, they pay that. When they're still, you know, first, the union there, yeah, right after union too, come in, then log haulers have to sign a contract. When they disappear, they don't have it.

words: A cubic foot?

And if there was the later years, you know, when first there was, you see, and when the later years, if they had longer haul you have to the river, then get more paid. And they have nothing to do with the log cart. Some of the good thing, you know. And then after that, you know, they don't have the sign, they couldn't draft it, not needed, you see.

Yeah. So I thought they were pretty good for the next year. And the log cart, you know, the company had. You give me just an idea about come from here, we talked about Mandy, that's Oscar's class. Oh. He was only nine, at 29, you know. Can you find me here? I don't know where I put my...

words: I believe that one there. There was two. But there was supposed to be one more. She went to more schools because she was the teacher. But then there was... No, it's... That's a girl you know, with the Bronx. Her dad was a rich man. He was a logger in the diesel. Ha-ha, he was this minister. He was the highest church coherer. Church coherer, yeah. Well, then he had been...

words: The one Lutheran, Lutheran. It usually was the Lutheran Church, but Mama was telling it. I knew these people and they don't live too far away. They were... I don't know what it was. They were in Lutheran. And they were... One or two of your kids went to come from Meresen. But I don't know which of my brothers. But being they were in Lutheran, they couldn't be confirmed. They could go to come from Meresen and learn. And the minister... Mama said that the minister said that you ask Mama and Daddy if I can baptize you. Then they could be confirmed. That was Wiecka.

words: So that was the difference. They weren't baptized.

words: Yeah, and when they were baptized, they were Lutheran. They were Lutheran. And they were Lutheran, of course. So it wasn't that they had... But the parents belonged to a university where I should know so well. I knew those people. So the young... Mama said that the kids cried too because they wanted to be confirmed. But the others... And the minister said that if he asked them if he can do it, then they could be confirmed. This confirmation, that was more like a school, you know. But when they confirmed, to be confirmed, that was different. But now, I guess, it's lots of different denominations.

words: Different, yeah.

words: Either Baptist or Methodist or something like that, they were those people. I remember, you know, one of the poets, you know, can I mean, you know, the preacher had... No, that was in the service, you know. And the other fellow went and told the minister that he pinched him in the bench, you know. The minister told him that he had to study again. There was a sudden delay. He told the boy, you go home. And the boy took off. Oh yeah, he was a poet. No human could do that, you know. It would be good when we were in competition. When they come back next Saturday. Thapra will never come back, but he did. He said, you go home. But now it's lots of... Now I heard the saying, and if I come home now, I'm going to ask him to find out more about things. He went home and took it for granted to us. He'll write down before we go, you see, but we're going to ask him about it. That's the only thing to do, is that I come home and leave you with a doubt. That was one thing, maybe I told you, the heart was... When Charles was born, and they used to take them and go and have them baptized, but if that kid was kind of weak or not feeling so good, they got someone to come and baptize them temporarily. Right in the house. Yes, I was at my godfather's place when they had a little baby that... And she died too, but they had a neighbor, Christian neighbor, woman, but they couldn't get the ministers out. I was there when that little girl was baptized, but that was there when they had to be baptized. They should be baptized. I remember home, you know, they come from the Norwegian border, and they had a team, you see, the preacher come over here, you see. And I sat one day, then they baptized them, the kids, you know. Well, they had to come over home, it was in wintertime, so they went to the home and they were changing on the babies, and they had coffee with them, and they have something else, and they went to the schoolhouse right there, where I lived. And then they come back there, and they had houses, you know. There were a lot of people in the house. But they had that idea years ago, I know the answer to that, you know, because I know it was five weeks before I baptized. I knew it wasn't that bad, it used to be, otherwise, that baptism, it took in the wintertime and cold and all that, they took those kids. And instantly, horses rode in this land and took them to the minister, and maybe it was far to the church, to the parsonage, but it could be. And then they said, I remember some men, they said, oh my, weren't they lucky when they went and hadn't baptized because that kid died on the way home. Yeah, it could be too cold too. Cold, you know. It could have, it could have, we never thought about it, it could have, we never lost the cold.
