words: That could be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that he was telling the people he knew who were from Armand in Minnesota to come out with him. And they came out, quite a few came out together, is what. Yeah, that could be a way, you know.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, oh yes. But Per Johansson, did I ever see him, but was he dead? No, he didn't come here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you know the Bohman family, or Ole Bohman's family over in Spain? Did you know the family?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We were two Swedish boys. I think it was the part we lived. We could have seen them more, but Mama and Papa, the moods that we gathered for, we bought this place where we lived. Other side of the tours. That's why we sat most by ourselves there, and the other was a bit, so much there, that we were up, so we saw them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that you were telling me about how they changed the name?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, yeah, Bertzl Bohman, Ole's son, he called me up one time. Because he thought that I should know things that he studied and started thinking about, and you know, for one thing, I was, of course, younger than for another thing. If I had lived there, I was born in Troy, so I wasn't up there so much. But he called me and talked to me about things, and then he said, do you know how they had got Bohman? Because his name would have been Ole Olssen. And they were just mixed up, the meal was mixed up. They had trouble all the time because it was too many, Ole Olssen, when they made. So he had picked up three names and wrote to his dad, sent those names, wrote to his dad, and told her what was with the meal and all. So he said, which of those names should I take? And Bohman was one of them, and his dad said that he should take that. Bertzl told me that. He lived in Troy. I never heard anything like that at home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the Bohman family very well off in Sweden?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yes, they were. I didn't mean that maybe they weren't anything. People had reached to the head, you know. But, yeah, consider to the head. Many of the others, they were. They were well off. And Ole Bohman and Axel Boomers did good when they came here because Ole started a bank. And they had that lumber company, so they were kind of a little more to go ahead and rest up, etc.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then, perhaps they had an advantage because they had some money when they came over here probably.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I don't know about that. I can't say that, but we know in time they would get that. I'm sure I shouldn't say that either, but I think they should get from their home. Yeah, so Mrs. Arthur Anderson, she was the sister of Ole Bohman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mrs. Arthur Anderson was Ole Bohman's...?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: She was the sister of Ole Bohman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, this was his present wife or his first wife?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You mean Arthur Anderson? Yeah. That was his first wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She has died since.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: She died from cancer. So he remarried. So they lived over here where my kids lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you first got married, were you thinking of coming over to the United States then? Did you think you might come?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't know what I thought. Of course, I was here, so I wanted to go here to America. Otherwise, I wouldn't have gone to America.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How soon did he leave Sweden after you got married?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, it wasn't long yet. It wasn't long at all. And John Markham came home from here and they started talking. There was all that, cooked up all that going in. And then they said, they talked to John Markham and he was working. And that's what Oscar was used to at home. So they talked and talked. And of course, you know for John Markham, it was kind of hard here. I was his sister. So we didn't want to talk so that you make him go, but he told how it was when Oscar wanted to know. So he said that, you know, I want to go to America, he said. And now, because it was papers that had to be filled in, we could just go and buy a ticket right away at the time. I said, what was all that? What was all that we have to have to go from home to America? What paper was that? We have to go to the minister, you know. Oh yeah. The minister to get paper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The pastor, you mean?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: And then we have to go to the other one for the American line, the Swedish line, what we call it. And he wrote into the king. Yeah, for him. For me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is this, the line? What line, you mean?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: When we come to America, I went on the Swedish line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean the boat?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He was an agent for that line, you see, so he got paid. So I went to him, he lived in Tósby, and he wrote to the king so I can leave. But you see, sometimes you know he can stop that, you see, when he won't stop. I've been in the army in Sweden and I don't know, I was in for three years, half an hour, he's never done with that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In and out of what, the army?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, in the army, in Pántirí, everything. And then here I was in there and I think I had to put my number on and see what had happened in the army. And then I got back up, but I can leave the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had to have the king's okay.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, king's okay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any trouble on the American side to get in? No.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, what about the Germans? I asked, wait, no, we have to go to Gothenburg, the American Consul and the doctor. And the doctor, he couldn't talk Swedish and he had a nurse, so it was targeted for him, see. And I remember I was in, there was one thing being here many times, and he asked him, and I don't know why he said that, and that man told me that, you haven't taken out the citizen papers in America. No, he said, I've been going back and forth. He said, it won't be what he said, you know, I don't understand him, he said, he talked to the doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I don't understand, why did he say that I was going back and forth?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, he said I've been going back and forth between America and Sweden, that they haven't taken out the citizen papers. Was that the doctor? Huh? Who was it that said that? I don't know him, he was in the same time I was in. Oh, you heard that. Yeah, I heard that. Then I asked him how he said, I said, I couldn't understand why he talked to the doctor, but that American doctor, he couldn't talk Swedish to him. When he had the lady there, a nurse, and she talked to the doctor, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the doctor say anything to you about coming to America?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, he never said nothing, he was okay, see, things like that. When you have to see the doctor, he's checking everything, so you're alright before you can go. One boy, I don't know how that happened with him, if he can go or not, I don't know where he was. So he had that other Swede, you know, he was older, you know, he asked the Swede, you know, and then he had to talk Swedish to the boy, you see, but oh, I don't know where he was. When I saw the boy after that, I don't know where he was, I don't know him, you know. Many of us was in there. No, if it has been for John or he'll come home, my brother, come home, he would never think about it. You, I never think about it, but I had one fellow I worked for, and he is, he is, he is, he is, he was a paper in Torsby, you know him, his name is Hendrickson, I worked for his dad, and I told him right into the Carnarian line, there was some place in Sweden, you know, an idiot for that, and he how much the cost, and he did, there was very much the cost to come in over the bar in to Hendrickson, and then you come in and come here, you see. There was very much the cost, there was quite a bit more coming here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she said that it was Hjalmar that got you interested in the idea, that's the way it happened, Hjalmar told you about it.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Hjalmar, he did, and I said, I talked to him, I always said, don't go to Canada, go into America, and he said, you like to work in the woods, in the round trolley, there's all kinds of work. He said, there was, I got plenty of it. Hjalmar, he come and said that, that now he think he's going to go to America, so saying now he said, because if I get to go, then you can say no, of course, it was pretty, it took pretty long for some to get all that straightened out to go. Eric Nelson, he used to own the place, you know, in Grandin? Oh yes. That place Eric Nelson, and he, Hjalmar wrote to him, like probably you did, but he going to get them papers, I think he sent papers to somebody else, so Eric Nelson went to Moscow, to Audra Nielsen, he was a, probably, you know what I'm calling it, he fixed out the paper, and Eric signed the paper, and some other, one we named Rudolph, he then knows, he made it, and he said, one more, and he sent that paper that I come in, some to work on the farm, and they were easily come in that week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They sponsored you, they said you could have a job when you came over, right?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, yes, yes, and I have to have so much money to come in here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have that much money?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I had it all in my pocket. Hjalmar?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Ja, yes. I don't have it. No, I don't. But, and then I was, Hjalmar, I wasn't a bit worried, because sometimes it took them so long, before all this papers and all this was, so I thought maybe, and some didn't get to go, I don't know why, if they weren't. Well, that went faster for me, he come to come here, where he come to work on the farm. Only like the head farm. So I wasn't a bit worried, I compared that he could go. That was so unexpected. I went so fast, Susan. Oh, your joy, how fast it went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then,

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I didn't say no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't say no? No. Could you have said no? Did he say no?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't think he had said no, but I think he had gone anyway. Should have heard the song, things Hjalmar told him about. No, anyway, I never said that. Then I worked one day, I worked one monthly troy, then I went to the woods. Yeah, but wasn't that done, staying troy? Well, I took how they was. I could have went with a carpenter, see. I worked a little bit with him, all the earrings, his name. And Joonilsson, you know, he come five, six weeks ahead of me, but I never know him in Sweden, see. And he was a carpenter from Sweden. And I said to the earrings, Hjalmar, I believe I'm going to quit. You work in the woods. My voice said, you don't quit. Well, I said, I don't really know how to do that work, I said. Hjalmar, I got all kinds of work for you, you can work with me all summer. And don't turn my bed to the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why is that done?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Hjalmar turned my bed to the woods, I could not learn to build houses. He was. That's what I could have done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you wanted to go in the woods, you knew how to do that.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, yes, you're right. I knew how to do that. And I really made more money. You would. Did you? Oh, yeah. Well, he didn't have much paid in our company. You know why? And you up here. Now you come, you come in 28. I was making a bedroom for part of the company in mountain. You could make money. It was eight dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing, on the section?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, we made the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The line?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, we made the railroad bed. And then come the steel gang and put steel on, ties and steel on top of the ties. And we made good. Ole Bohman, of course, come as soon as he knew I come, he come to talk to me and say, and he said that he makes pretty good. He said, I never thought about making anything. But he said how much was it he said to me, it didn't sound so awful much, but I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, you know, he was back in Sweden, Ole Bohman?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, as soon as I come to Troy, you know, he came to hear things were in Sweden and see how I was. Oh, he was awful good, I miss Ole. He was very good, many times. Oh, he was real good. He was real good. He could be with any kind of people. He was a religious man, he could always be with everybody. But he was in the bank business, you know. They could come in, drinking people too, you know. And one fella, you know, I didn't think he was in Ole Bohman, the Mulsons boy, you know. He said, he went into Ole, Ole told me inside, I have to get money. He said, no, but I didn't need money. Why don't you do like your brother, he put money in a bank here and you're going to loan money. He said, I have to have money. Well, they gave him money. And he always paid. He took chances to help us, he's lying. To help the men. He knew them. Yeah, of course he knew them. They paid. You could only have money here, you're going to loan. You know Ole Bohman's dad, he was a Christian person. And he was so, if they needed help, they should help. But he didn't have a bank business or anything, but even where he lived on the farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he tried to help other people in Sweden?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yeah, not business much, but they come and ask for things. He was in the county. He was in the county, worked for the county and people come and they have help. But he had to get something to eat and give them some money. Same with the highway commissioner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do in the county, in Sweden?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, they do the same in the county here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what his job was? Was he like a county commissioner?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, he was a university, but same, I think they call for us. They have to go to him, to get help. And some of them, they turn in the head, you know. But he had to take them first over there, to his home. Then if he got a place for them, to send them away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had to put them up at his house?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, put them up in the house. It was almost crazy, so he had to tie them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was talking to Anna about how high he was in Sweden. Was he very high ranking?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He was the highest one in the county, because he had that job. He was a rich man. Yeah, rich, but I don't think he was a good person. He was a high rank, on that kind of board. Oh, yeah, on his board. He was that kind of board he had. That's why he was high. No, but he was a good heart. I know that. You know that, you know. They help them, then they come and ball them up, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? Who balled them up?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They're like, they have help. If they didn't get enough. Oh, they didn't get enough. I knew him. I remember. Mama always called him uncle. So we kids always called him uncle, too. But, so I saw him, but about the business and things. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he your mother's uncle? Huh? Was he your mother's uncle? Yes, yes. Well, didn't you tell me that you saw, was it Ole or Axel Bohman who came back when you were just a little kid and you saw him in Sweden? Yeah. Was it, which one, didn't you say? No, I don't know.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: The Y-X. I think it was Axel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He comes back.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You see, he, when I was home from visit, the words, he's a woman and neighbor and all that, but we can believe by ourselves, you see. But I remember when he was there to see us, because then he could tell when he come back here that he had seen us. So we cannot try to see our life. But all of us, all of us. I don't know. Young woman, you know, he had a time. Yeah, you know, when they had to stay. No, Ole was good when they come and told him how she, he was going to, cannot trust me when it was in some way with his income. How do you know? Then he thought it was there no much. And when Ole come and said, oh, he does pretty good. He have his check, but so and so much, he said. Well, I tell you, that was really good that I could work in the camp. See, we were bored when they come and we had the lowest check. They're all debt somewhere, about $180. And they were bored with all that. How much did you say? $180. Oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For how long? What? A month. That's not bad.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: It was good, huh? Really good. And we were called some. No, only Ole missed Ole a lot because Ole was in the camp and Ole was here alone. And I tell you, he come so many times after see how I met him. Oh, he helped me in the about the place here. He was in it with me to Moscow there. And we put in a bet on $350, I think it was. For about $3500, I was betting on the place. When I was sealed bet, see, there were $350, 10% to send. And then we took out the citizen paper. He was the witness. Oh, yeah. Herman Paulson. He said, no, he said, no, we're going to eat an Oscar. Oscar had to buy the dinner for us. I said, hey, we're okay. Oh, he was good. We all were good, but Ole, he come so many times. There was more in business too, you know, actually was too. It was really us, Ole, that cannot pass. And I loaned $1800, $1700 in the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From the bank?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's good. He gave you a good-sized loan.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, he did. Well, then he said one time, ask her if you can get a loan, you get the cheaper interest, he said. One day I worked against the bank. He said, man, I cannot like to help you, so you don't have to pay some of the interest. But then I went to Gustav Olsson, they call Olsson's dad. And I loaned from him $1700 and 6%. But then he cut down to 5% later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mr. Olsson did?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, oh, he did. Yes, he did. When he have to have a mortgage on the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So really then, Ole was sort of, he was like your advisor. Yes, I guess he advised me. I guess maybe he did, didn't he?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I don't know, but he got started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have helped a lot of people. I get the idea that the community, the people really depended on the goodness, his goodness.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, you bet. And then he did other things too. Oh, maybe that was many that didn't like, you know, it's all, it is never one that disliked of everyone. Well, then they put up some and you know, you can work for them. And many that come from Sweden, you know, what kind of green, you know, can talk, you know, what nice they can work for them. They can talk, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work for him at all? No. Or did you go right to Potlatch?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I went right to Potlatch. But they, they, they kind of close up the mills there, you know. So they don't tell us that they had quite a few mills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So maybe it was more earlier days?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, a lot of earlier days, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I was asking Anna too, do you think that because his father was pretty well off in Sweden, that that gave the boys some money when they came over here to start with, to rest?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, yes. There were days when the woman was home, 1905, and he went home and come there to get money for that, he had money coming, do you see? And then he started in the bank business. Oh, yes, I have. Yes, and the bank, first bank of Troy started in 1905.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He borrowed that money from his father?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I think he had the money coming, he had money. I see. I don't think he had money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his father still alive then?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, oh yeah, he was still alive. But they were like, older, so they kind of wanted to talk to their dads, like he wrote about the dam and all that, so I guess. Well, you some don't like, you know, you can't please all the people, you know. Probably he had advised them, but probably don't went so good, but then they went to a couple of them and bawled him out, too, when they should never do that. And I know one fella did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard that too, but a banker is in a difficult position, he's got a lot of responsibility for things go right or things go wrong.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, well, like knowing that when the bank grows, you know, what do you say? I'll tell you. Many banks closed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, even the bank of Troy closed for a while. Do you think he was very worried when the bank closed? Did you see him after he was loaded?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: They were worried excellently. When you see it. The woman had a farm, over here, that way, the ... had a farm, so I heard her say this, the woman's wife told him, we can go to the farm. And then they go there. No, he said, I don't care about my money, man, there are other monies I want to go with that. I have money in the bank, there are other people. Other people. I don't care about my money. So they lived in town, all the women, and Mrs. and Pat, they had the farm there, but they never lived on that farm since we came, did they? No. No. There hasn't been any round of it. But when I come, we had nothing. Not a thing. And there was one of us was at the moment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Good health and like to work. Good health and like to work.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, couldn't work. They were, they depend. But where we lived in the little old house at Eric Nelson's we didn't pay any rent. But we did pay the cost the painting of the house and things like that. And I got about milk from Mrs. Nelson and pigs and things like that. And milk was 50 cents. It was a gallon. I don't think you got 50 cents a gallon that time. And the eggs wasn't much, you see, it didn't take much. And it was no water in that house, so we pulled water from the valve. And we wrote, Mrs. Nelson and I, we washed together because it took less water. So we wrote clothes and washed together. And we worked together so much, she was a good cheer. So it wasn't expense. We couldn't have had that stuff, but it was earnings either. No. But it went. We had food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it must have been costly to come over. Both of you had to come over and pay the fare.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: So he came and he borrowed money for his fare. And then he paid that up for us. I paid that up and I said, money for her, you know, and it was only on one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You sent the money for her too?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, and the baby. And the baby. You had to pay some for him, you know, on the boat. I don't know why. He must have gone free, because I remember when I come to New York. It might be different. When I come to New York, and you see that was first part, not the first year, we got off the boat first of July and stayed overnight there. And next thing you know, people were traveling. The train was so packed, so it was so hot. Oh, and I, there I, but I had an aunt that lived in Tacoma, and she had been home, so I was, I was coming with her. So, you know, she knew what they were talking about, but I didn't know anything about what they were talking about. And I sat, and I sat my girl beside me, and her conductor, and what was the conductor, but they come and said, I should pick, hold that kid. Yeah, oh yeah, I didn't know what they said, but my aunt said that you have to hold her in your lap. And I heard she said something to him, but I don't know what was, she said to me that, but she can't sit and hold the baby, because I was far from New York, and I just arrived to Georgia to sit on the train with the baby. But he said to her, she told me, what about, she said, she, this baby has impaired anything for a while. For who? About the people that stood waiting and what to see, so I had to pick her up and hold her, and I didn't know anything, but he said that my aunt told me what it was. So it must be, it must be some I appeared for the ride that way, I didn't know. How the boat was, how the boat was, I couldn't say. Oh, the boat, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the boat trip, was that a hard trip going across the ocean?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, across the ocean, of course. I and a friend that I had was neighbors. We were a little bit seasick, and the kids don't, I had them right, and she was really sick, but she was, he was satisfied with things. If I had had Vernon, he would never have been satisfied to be in that little room, that would have been more, but she was satisfied to talk to her, and she was satisfied to be there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay in just a little house, a little room you had?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, you know those places on the boat, it isn't much, and there was the boat. That was the bottom of the top of the boat? Yeah, on both sides. But I remember that was it, neighbors treatment, me, that has been in this country, was home, and he come back, so he could have some company on the way, but of course it was better for me, and for him, he get along, but that, you know, he was really good. My neighbor that was in the same room there, I was, we lay down because we wasn't really feeling good, and Lily and Rich, she was satisfied to bunk around with things there, so that one good, but then that boy that was neighbor to me, he come, he was disgusted because he said, you aren't sick here, all you that was the other neighbor friend, get up and go up on the deck, we didn't want to go, we grew up in those bad, but he got us out of bad boat, and got born in a charm, and you go, we go up there, and now you're going to have a glass of beer, and we said, no we never have tasted beer, we just heard it, you were seasick, now you said you were seasick, now you better drink this, he said at the end, why Dwight, glass of lones, and he didn't give up, we really wasn't sick, but we were in born with too lazy to mow around, so he never drink, I'll never forget that, that we had,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make you feel worse or better?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, better. It did get better. I tell you, I never have tasted beer, but Both of us said we never tasted beer, and he said, you were seasick, now you drink this, and he stood there and we had to drink it, but it didn't taste so bad, and we thought it would, so anyway that was my first beer drink, it was on the boat, oh yeah, we, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, we talked about this trip over, and I remember you told me that before you went, that you heard that people were telling you, did somebody tell you that women didn't have to work in America?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, oh, it was one of the neighbors, a friend of mine, someone said that, she come in and saw me also, I was visiting my cousins, and there she come, and I knew really well, and she said, hey, are you going to America, and I knew that you're going to America, you're going to do things, she said, sit in a chair with your feet on the foot, and they dressed them and everything, and I said, how am I going to dress that no women works? Because,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said, who's going to dress?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I said, Elma, I said to her, her name was Elma, I told her that all I had to do was to sit there in a chair, and have my feet on the footstool when I got to America, and they dressed me, she said. But Elma said, who's going to dress me, you know women working on the way. So, I got, I find out little bit, there was lots of cows in the barn, after little bit. So, that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were going to leave Sweden, did you have very mixed feelings about leaving, about leaving where you'd grown up, or were you just looking forward to America?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, I guess I was going. I guess there was no other way, it wasn't one way or another, when I got the ticket and all that, it was to go, and then I got to live at the Eric Nelsons there. You know, there were, not that I mean Swedish people are better, but I could talk to them, and they were way into the Eric Nelsons there, and I was like home there, and the language you walk between, there was my little, I lived in the old house, and the big, and she did, you know, she was home there too. So, if I have sat in a house there, here in this country, and couldn't have talked to many, you know, not a person, and not understood a word, you know, that would be awful tough. I wouldn't know what I would have done. And Earth was there with his team, and went to town, and I rode with them, and got groceries, things like that. Oscar was in the camp, so he wasn't home. So, I couldn't have had a better place, you know, and I, I was... The difference for me though, going to camp and there's no nobody, and you sit in the table, you know, you have to call for what you're eating. See? You have to call for it? Yes, if you're going to have so and so, you call for it, and I don't know what I call it. See? Some of that food, you know. No. So, like here, I was sitting here, you sit in the center, there were three on each side, you can reach over, one sitting there, but you couldn't reach over on the side, either side. Nobody had to go on there. You have to ask for it, and I don't know what I called it. No. How did you get it? Well, finally I reached over, and some, they know what they know what, they were just coming over, so then they helped me, you know, some of them, they sent to me. They sent to me, stopped, and I don't like to eat, but I eat a tenement. No, he'll let me take it. Left for the better time. Oh, that's not easy. That trouble I didn't have, because I come right, I stayed there, so. We meet somebody on the road, you know, we were kind of afraid that they want to talk to us. I know that, Gerst Berg, you know, them come after me, you see, they come the same day, but they come a great time, and he walk from the camp one time, and he had one behind, you see, and he walk all he could, you know, he thought he was going to catch up, and he didn't talk to him, he couldn't talk to him, you know. He said, I had an awful time till I get away from that man. That time that Eric Nelson, best friend was Eric Nelson, went on a little marriage, he was saved, and I was alone, and that peddler came, and he was used to go there and devote things from him, and I had a hair, and he was, I couldn't, I didn't understand him, and he, and I never even talked, and I didn't understand, he didn't of course understand me, but I said, I heard the same saying, and they were on the little marriage side, I said to him, just like that, little married saying, I said, I remember that, so the peddler went there. He said to Eric, but he got this, he said, you know the world kind of people you have at home, can't either understand at all. You were said, you have come from Sweden, just three days ago, that was an excuse, yeah. That was easy. Oh, but I have to say. I wasn't afraid, I was afraid of people, you know, but they started talking to me, and I can, I don't understand you see. But it was, it doesn't take too long, we don't know what to talk yet, like that. What? But, but I say, it doesn't take too long to, that we understand very bit about coffee, and, and, and things like that, it doesn't take too long, but it was starting to get along with everyday things. If you come, if you come here younger, you know, so you come out with them boys, see that difference, then older you know you can come out and go to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hard to learn the language.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, if you're younger you know easy to learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, did, did the lumberjacks used to joke, give you a hard time, because of that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: There, there, we, some, we were going some of the days, you know. Yes, yes, but one day, what can I, what can I say about it? I don't know very much about it. He don't know very much about it, but he tells me I understand very much. You see, there was a nigger family, you know, Wells, you know, they were playing, and they told me, you go back there and see nigger Mary, otherwise you'll never be citizen here. That man, he teased me, you know, but I couldn't understand him. But when I was in there, I didn't mean I started right away, but I had in my mind, I didn't want to be in this country if I can't become a citizen. I didn't feel, I don't know, I was just like sitting there and didn't belong any place. Well, you belonged, you belonged to that country where you come from, so long as you haven't taken your citizen papers. Yeah, but that was there and I was here. No, when you see that, we come from Sweden, it was Swedish citizen, born in Sweden. Well, so long as we don't took a paper here, we were still Swedish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you felt a little like a person without a country.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, that's what I said to you, you know, what you and my dad used to do. But I wanted to take out the paper and ask because I don't know what, they didn't know and all that to be there to you, and I said, you can do what you want. I was here when we were having seventeen years old. Seventeen years we was here and we took the papers. Been here seventeen years. How come? We waited that long. Well, from twenty-seven to forty-four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you waited so long?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, we waited that long so I could understand what your idea was that I saw them talk about it. Oh, we had a, we had a few lessons to learn. Well, we have to, oh, you bet, we have to study. We get the Constitution of United States.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you had to study to pass, to become a citizen?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, you have to study there. And then what time comes of year? And then, then there were examiners, see. Who? In examiners you were some asker first before we come from the earth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, come from the earth.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: And then all the women were with, you know, and Herman Paulson, and there were witness, you know, things, you know, he asked them. And he asked me one question here, some, some, he asked me, don't, don't, don't the president, don't keep pointing them at senators? No, I said, I don't think so, they might have done it a long time ago, but they don't do it now. People vote for them. And he, he tried to, to see if I know of anything. Trick, trick, yeah. Yeah. But that's a good. You know, he never said anything. That was a good thing that you knew then. No, I know, no. No, it was so cute that that man, that asker things, it was, we had to go up, the judge was there, but that was, and he was so young and I felt we were old people. And at least then he said that, you know, what should we do with these kids? He said, and we were older than he was, I think, at least I felt that. But of course we passed. Well, then you know, then we had the ex-husband too. Oh yeah, he asked. He asked, well, he'd been in the other one before, he said, he asked me if I'd been taking out the first paper before. Yes, I said, I have. Why don't you keep on with it? No, I said, I was afraid I couldn't make it, I said. Well, you're gonna make it now, he said. He was really good. I have to say, I said no, they know everything about things, you know. I talked the first paper, the fool made me go take the first paper and I don't know nothing, you see. After seven years, that's what we wrote. He talked, the judge, Clark, Walter Clark. Yeah, he used to be in New York. He talked to him first and then he talked to me and I guess it must be paper that he had gone and come here first draft. So he said, and you tagged him along over here, he said to me. Yes, I did. I'll never forget that. That was a nice year, though. And those words, I remember what you said, and you tagged him along over here. Oh, you were very nice. Because we didn't come the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you take out your first papers when, soon after you got here?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, it was a couple of years, I think. I see them upstairs here yet. Eric Nelson fooled me to do that. I went up behind the courthouse in Moscow and the judge was Oscar Nilsson, he was a Swede, It was easy to get the first paper that way, Well

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't have to pass a test to get the first paper.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, no, not the first paper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just to become a citizen.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: But then, over seven years, they were too old. The deputy asked me if I could keep on with it. I said, I'm afraid I couldn't make it. But I did see. But you were going to make another. But I did say that I wanted to do it and end with paper. Man, there were papers that were here, there were papers that were here 40 years before I took the paper. Somebody never took it. I don't know how. I was so unsure. I talked to her now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everything is here. You talked to who?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, just Mrs. Nelson. She was born here, but I told her what I feel like. I wish I should like to be a citizen when I'm here because I felt like I had a new body in one way. And then again I felt like I was sitting there. So I guess she's, I don't remember exactly what she said, but we talked more than once. And then we started and it went alright. Like I said, woman you know, he took his paper. I think you can get the paper in five, it has to be five years here. So you already asked him why you took the paper to the correct. Five years. Yes, he said, I like to have protection. So he said, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a good...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I actually told him, he told me himself. That was my identity. You need protection if you're a citizen here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're protected against what?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Well, a lot of things you know don't come up. You know, your protection, see they can't probably help you, they probably work for you, things like that. But if you don't sit this and you do things like that, probably don't do so right, they don't care about that. Yeah, that wasn't that much. But I remember John Clark. He was a nice man. He was father-in-law to Frank Church. Oh. Yes, I don't know that for an he died. And I read that in the paper. And Frank Church is a good man. Yeah, but son-in-law, did you say that? Yeah, Frank Church was son-in-law to Clark. Oh, yeah, that was Frank Church. I thought it was the other way and I knew that couldn't come because George Clark was old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you took the train out and you had to sit up on that train all the way, it wasn't a very... No, it was a pretty rough trip.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I tell you, it was hot. So it couldn't be worse. And then so many people that when I got towards Spokane, or closer to Spokane, then it cooled off at night. I sat with that kid because it was so crowded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You probably couldn't sleep much on that train.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, I guess I couldn't sleep any way. But I had her laying beside me. I sat and let her lay. Yeah, they got room, you know. But it went all right. But I knew on the boat, you see, there the kids could run around and there they could be on the... I know what Roger Vernon, our boy, was so active. He wouldn't have... But Ingrid was satisfied if we gave her some papers to draw on and if she had some pictures to look at, she was satisfied. Well, there would be more kids on the boat, too. Oh, yeah. But my goodness, they had swings on it so they could swing. But who in all the world dares to... They had stairs up and stairs down and it goes... We had to have eye on the kids there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Troy when you finally pulled into the station and finally got to Troy? Did you think after all the things that you'd seen, did you have any first impressions of the town of Troy?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Troy? I tell you that I wasn't a soldier to me. You probably were thinking about the big city. Yeah, I couldn't say that. I thought they were bigger than they were. But how came I thought about Troy? Yeah, I know. But I come to Troy and I have written so many times for my brother's address and for his address, Troy. And here was the deal with Troy and nobody was there meeting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: And I tell you how it happened. So my brother that lived in Troy, that met him, what was it they were? They were the Spokane. They were the Spokane to meet her. And she never come. She never come. She stayed in one night in New York and two nights in Minneapolis. And I thought she was going to go direct to Troy. Yeah, in New York. So we stayed in Spokane. It was no way I was to... We come down from Spokane, then she was in Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do after you got off the train?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I tell you. I got off. Of course the conductor didn't let me off. And there was one man at the depot in Troy. Yeah, they said it was an old Norwegian man that used to go down and meet the train. No, he took the people. And he had no one to meet. So I come down and I took my suitcases and John kind of sitting there and I had him right on my arm. I was going to know he didn't let me off until he had talked to that man. And then, of course, when I got to go off, he took one suitcase stuck in that man's hand. But he was Norwegian so I could talk to him. But he didn't like to be there and meet me. He looked so kind of mad and all that. Yes, he did. And the other women in Troy said he had a... He was an older man but he had a video across the railroad to go and see. He said maybe he was this man. He made it strong, of course. He said maybe he was on his way over there to go and see her. But he went with me and he looked mad, awful mad. He took my suitcase and I had some other things to get. But then we met someone. Hjalmar had, I have to say, a picture of a man that Hjalmar was friends with. And we met him. And I asked him if he was in Schonig and Alfred Olsen was in there. Yes, he said that that Norwegian man said, Now you can take care of this one and give him the suitcase.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You recognized him from the picture?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: From the picture. Hjalmar's picture. But they didn't call him Alfred Olsen. The friends had a nickname, Schonig, because he was from Schonig in Sweden. He was from Soutenpater, Sweden. When he was born in the back east, he was his owner. Yes. And they started the group, you know, that they worked together. They started calling him Schonig. That was all I knew, so I asked him if he was in Schonig. Yes, he said. It was terrible for him, too, because my brother, excellent worker for Green's warehouse, had kind of vacation and walked. Schonig took his place at the warehouse. You see, they are tied down and they have a job. They are being home eating dinner. Because the train come in at 12. And that Norwegian was a mad man. He took off right away when he got there. Should we go? Because he talks Swedish. Ja, I said, if you can take me to Axel Bohman, because of course I knew then. Ja, he said he had this job to start in during the warehouse and all that. So that we walked up. And I had, you know, lots of young for her and Schonig in the suitcase and people at the store come out and look. You see, in all the work this was of the new Schonig, I couldn't imagine how he was involved. So he took me up to Axel Bohman. Not Schonig, but there Axel Bohman had come in, an office or something, so he took me home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Axel did?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yes, as soon as I got there. I have had enough. I had a headache and I threw up on the train. Because I was worn out, you know, after all that. The boat ride and all. And I heard there was Indian women on the train. And I went in, I was there and I threw up like everyone in the washroom. And my mom must be here, she said. I was in Swedish, she told me I was one of them. I heard her. And the Indians, women took her and the athletes and they left her and played with them. So things went alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the train?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Of course when I got the train up, I sat on the ...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then Axel Bohman took you straight out to the Nelson's place?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, you see when dad would have been far from town out there. But you see I had my brother and his wife at the Oscar where they come home. So then we went there to stay all night. That is this brother that lost his legs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was young then. So then they had arranged, so it was already all arranged that you would be staying at the Nelson's?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: So he went to meet Oscar when he came. And he worked for Green, of course, warehouse. So we ran them on his noon hour because it was noon and the train come in. So we ran and met three or many times before Oscar. It's hard to tell exactly the day. So he was there. And we had to try to see if he came back then when the day Oscar came. Because Oscar took him home. So there was this Axel said to me, I thought maybe it will be a little, little, I don't know what he called him. I had come that big man out of the train. He said Oscar can ask bigger than you expect. So, no, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you first got to the house, Deb and Nelson's place, what did you think of that? Did you think it was a nice place right away? I mean, did you feel at home there or did it take a while?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I tell you, I don't know. It was that old farmhouse they had. It was only the big house they had. But there was Mr. Nelson, American, and there was someone there to talk to. He said, Grant, alright. And Oscar went to talk to us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She spoke Swedish. Mrs. Nelson spoke Swedish. Oh, gee, old town.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, that court team lost his legs. That's too bad. Yeah, that was too bad. But he's quite a happy guy. In a way, he's glad that it turned out so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were there.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I think about that trip. But it's funny, I never was sorry that I... I was alright when I got there. I had a place to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Oscar stay at home for very long or did he have to go right off to the...

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: He went right away to work. Because he thought it was so important. Of course, it was too. Because I guess... He made some mind tickets, I guess it wasn't paid for me. So, and he always, in that time, he didn't want to skip there and he kept on to the mix. But I wasn't alone, you see. I had there Nelson's there. And they had friends and they talked to each other and they came and it was nice. So, at the back, I am just fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were the other people that you met? Their neighbors?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yeah, Mr. Nelson's brother and sister lived close by there and they came. And other of their friends. And of course, their friend, they come. They didn't come for much to see me, but they come over and I was introduced and it didn't take long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they probably wanted to see the new family too.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, yes, could be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Curious about them.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, of course. So, I went, yes, fine. Yes, fine, I wasn't. In English, he felt a woman, she talked Swedish to everybody. Almost all of them understood. Of those neighbors and friends. Eric, he was so crazy about kids and it's Easter. He called her a boy. He said, Mama, where did you get that boy from? I'm not the boy, she said, I'm a girl. Then after a little bit, he has to see him, she got some answers, he yelled at him. I told him I'm a girl. Oh, he was so good. He liked kids. And, you know, no, it wasn't so bad. But if I would have said out in the country, like that would be all by myself, with Ingrid, I don't know what I would have done. Because it wouldn't have been easy to, I didn't know if the idea of how people could come or anything. I had the urge to help us. I used to go out and milk, help the milk. There were so many cars, cost the milk. When I decided to leave, I didn't have to. I went out and milked and I was just trying to almost win. No, they all were good to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you must have seen them every day? You must have spent time with them every day then? Oh yeah. A lot of visiting back and forth.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: When we pulled, I guess I told him, I pulled him in that cart. And Ingrid didn't have to write in it and she wants to. He made a little thing and a talk. It was weird. And Ingrid wanted to write that thing so bad but she didn't dare to. And then with the hay in the bottom of it, I said, sit down. I pulled. No, no, you sit with me. Who should pull then? Couldn't have Mrs. Nelson to pull me. So, I said to her, I said to Hilda, I said, I'm calling. If she wants to get to the right, maybe she'll be there. She's afraid to ride the wrong. Hilda went there and Ingrid, there and Ingrid rode. I said, she said, my bad, people go by here and they say that you pull. And they say, no, Mrs. Nelson, that's the weirdest woman to pull her around. I wish nobody thought about that, about Hilda's misery. That she said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: We had lots of fun. We had lots of fun. They were so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that she helped you learn a lot about how to get along in America, in American ways? I mean, did she sort of tell you things that you didn't need to know?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I learned to cook and bake. We didn't do it the same way there as here, so I got to learn that from Hilda.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was different here than it was?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, it was different. Of course, Swedish people would eat and drink anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm curious about that. What was the differences that you learned here from the way you cooked over there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, I like baking. That was the difference at home. We baked that big flatbread, big rontings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Flatbread?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah. And here they made loaves. Of course, the cough bread, that wasn't different because we made that at home. Of course, it was different recipe, have cookies and things about it. But that was a minor thing. Hilda baked them. And if I want to learn, I could because she was a good cook. Oh, she was a good cook. And we had lots of fun Hilda. When it was cut in piece, and you bunched and sat on the mower and bunched them like that. And I thought, I said, no, I took a fork and asked her, you see, that wasn't enough work for me to sit there. I was young. I was used to work to sit there in that little house. And then we bought some at Hilda's. It was dark at my place. So I went there and shopped. And I did that the first day. And Hilda, of course, we were in for dinner at Hilda's. But the next day she said, no, I'm going out. And she had so much to do. They had so many cows. And she cooked so well and baked so much. She didn't have time. And she was old then and I wasn't old. So she said that, but no, I'm going out in the field, she said, because you weren't going to go there another day. And then I cooked in my poor way. I cooked the beans. So that was good for her, but not that good. She was satisfied. Everybody was good to me. Everybody was good to me. Send people, was friendly. And when he talks Swedish, that was good at the time. Because I didn't understand anything. Once they come and work in Spokane and had to start working for American people, they said they learn fast because they had to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this about working in Spokane?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: You didn't do that, did you? No, girls that come over and work, but had worked, you know, they hired maids years ago in Spokane. It was many from home that went to America and they worked in Spokane. But of course it was hard for them at first because they don't understand anything, but they learn though that way. But I had Swedish and Norwegian all around me. I didn't have to try even. So that's the way. But all of my sister and I, she worked there, and she said that when they come and pick up the long rib, not sure when they took the boss's soup, then they couldn't take. She said, I wasn't going to let that go. They forgot some place and the maid comes a certain time to pick it up. So when he got home, his soup wasn't cleaned at least. But she said, I thought he didn't know where it was to pick up things. But she said when I saw it, I thought, his soup, I'm not going to let go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She wasn't sure if they were going to bring it all back.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I guess she thought it was. Yeah, she said that. I wasn't going to let his soup go at least.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, she lived down here, but she went up there to work. Is that what it was, or did she live in Spokane?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: She come as a girl here from Sweden. And I guess the most ripe between, of course, I never was in on that because I never worked up there. But they said that of course it was girls that come to work and went to the city. In the country, they didn't hire girls much. So they were in Spokane, but there was many that worked in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you think that she still, her brothers were down here.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh yeah, they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would she come back down here when she wasn't working?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yes, she had someone. So, but they not told me that herself. She did. Then she worked in Spokane and they were going to come and her, my friend, she says, was gone. And about that, she said, no one thing I thought, I don't let them take any of these suits. She didn't either. So I guess there's many things that's funny that goes on. She worked in Spokane. I don't know. Then she married and lived here over at that place, Farmhairs, after life. A coal out time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it is.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: It's a coal. Oh, really? Oh yeah, that's what Anna said, she said that it predicted wind and coal tonight. I'm telling you, when Anna worked in Spokane, any of them left. They did suit, but they come to pick up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you didn't try to learn English at first? I just went and talked in Swedish. Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: I never got up to work, I should have. But there I had him written, then he was working. So I guess I had a little bit to live from the top. Like in the camp, you know, there's a lot of Swedes, you know. You know you hang around the Swedes, you know. One fella told me, you just hang around, there are Americans who can learn. Don't hang around, Swede. What did you do now, you don't understand nothing, Peter. No, but they say they learn faster if they get in on the American speaking people. They get in on it. They speak better all the time. You learn it quicker, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do that, or did you try to do that, or did you just stick with that?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: No, I hang around on Swedes. I bet he was... Having stories and things like that. I bet he was glad for every Swede he saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you must have seen quite a few Swedes in the woods, didn't you, there?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: In the camps, yeah. Most, there was a lot of Swedes that worked in the camp. Young boys, you know, that come over. They work in the woods. Oh, it wasn't, it wasn't, after all, that wasn't bad at all. They come home and you know that you hang around, they can talk American, they were sure you knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was the same thing with you, too, Anna. You must have spoken in Swedish, because the people all spoke Swedish around, right? Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: So you... Yeah, just the same. But I heard they said they do the best if they get among people that they have to try it, American.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long do you think it was before you started, you know, expressing yourself in English?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Oh, it was like, it was gradually. I would have wanted to go to school. I said that because I didn't understand how it was. I said that there over at Mrs. Eric Nelson, I said that. And I talked to someone that I wished I could go to school. I said that I was satisfied with the ordinary country grade school. I bet they said you can't do that. I can't see it, they have an old woman sitting there. No. All the kids. Long time ago, you can go to school and listen to them, you see. You can do it now. Well, but you know, like so many girls, they have school in Spokane, but Ingrid, she talks Swedish, but she wants the real Swedish, of course. When they learn from... Ingrid learned Swedish, and she learned American, she went to school, and she had known American before she went to school. Oh, yes. And same with Vernon. They learn from... They learn from boys and girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They learn from their playmates.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Playmates, oh dear. Oh yeah. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They probably spoke better than you did when they were kids.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: She went to Swedish school now, in Spokkäl, her daughter, and she said, I taught her no Swedish. She don't know no real Swedish. But knows her daughter-in-law, but she said, go get her, she went to school, and I can write to her, and she read the letters. And she can write back, and it's so easy, because the writing was worse. I could talk to her if we call in the phone, so when we got along fine, but that writing, and that's so bad, yeah, but I write Swedish. She learned. She learned when she took that class, night class, and Ingrid takes that. But she wasn't sure that she was right in wanting English. She didn't say that to him, but she asked us. They had their marking down, you know, printing down and all that. So she asked us. And she thought she was. And that, I say that, I think it, you know, shrink, shrink, I Sweden, that's a ham. And they were going to, when I, like the class was going to be a rabbit, and was going to explain it and all that. She said it was ham here. But the teacher said it was hamburger. And could that be possible?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't see how it could be both, because they're different things.

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: With ham, ham, that was shrinka in Swedish. And Ingrid knew that ham was shrinka. But the teacher said that instead of saying ham, they should say hamburger. And she asked us without saying anything about the school. And she said, what would ham be in Swedish? Shrinka, they call that the ham, that's shrinka. That's what I marked down, she said, at school. But she said it was supposed to be hamburger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you still write in Swedish? Do you still write letters in Swedish?

speaker: Oscar Thomason or Anna Thomason
words: Yeah, that's all I can draw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's all you can do? I was wondering.
